  • name (in path): string, required - name of the ResourceQuota
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the ResourceQuota
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuota): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Service ResourceQuota

Retrieve the compute quota details for the Skills microservice.

# Get Skills ServiceResourceQuota in YAML format
kubectl get resourcequota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: ResourceQuota metadata: name: skills-compute-quota namespace: skills-service labels: app: anihpj service: skills spec: hard: limits.cpu: "8" limits.memory: 16Gi pods: "20" requests.cpu: "4" requests.memory: 8Gi status: hard: limits.cpu: "8" limits.memory: 16Gi pods: "20" requests.cpu: "4" requests.memory: 8Gi used: limits.cpu: "4" limits.memory: 8Gi pods: "12" requests.cpu: "2" requests.memory: 4Gi
Example 2: Get User Service ResourceQuota with describe

View detailed quota information for User authentication services.

# Describe User Service ResourceQuota
kubectl describe resourcequota user-storage-quota -n user-service
Name: user-storage-quota Namespace: user-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- persistentvolumeclaims 4 5 requests.cpu 2500m 3 requests.memory 5Gi 6Gi requests.storage 45Gi 50Gi
Example 3: Get Author Service ResourceQuota in JSON

Retrieve Author service object quota in JSON format for programmatic processing.

# Get Author ResourceQuota as JSON
kubectl get quota author-object-quota -n author-service -o json
{ "apiVersion": "v1", "kind": "ResourceQuota", "metadata": { "name": "author-object-quota", "namespace": "author-service", "labels": { "app": "anihpj", "service": "author" } }, "spec": { "hard": { "configmaps": "10", "deployments.apps": "5", "pods": "15", "secrets": "10", "services": "5" } }, "status": { "hard": { "configmaps": "10", "deployments.apps": "5", "pods": "15", "secrets": "10", "services": "5" }, "used": { "configmaps": "6", "deployments.apps": "2", "pods": "8", "secrets": "5", "services": "3" } } }
Example 4: Get Location Service ResourceQuota with jsonpath

Extract specific fields from Location service quota using jsonpath.

# Get Location ResourceQuota hard limits only
kubectl get resourcequota location-priority-quota -n location-service -o jsonpath='{.status.hard}'
{"pods":"10","requests.cpu":"5","requests.memory":"10Gi"}
Example 5: Get JobPost Service ResourceQuota Usage

Check current resource usage against limits for the main JobPost application.

# Get JobPost ResourceQuota showing used vs hard
kubectl get quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service
NAME AGE REQUEST LIMIT jobpost-comprehensive-quota 5d pods: 28/40, requests.cpu: 9/12, requests.memory: 18Gi/24Gi, requests.storage: 100Gi/150Gi limits.cpu: 18/24, limits.memory: 36Gi/48Gi

get - read status of the specified ResourceQuota

Retrieve the status subresource of a ResourceQuota showing current usage.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the ResourceQuota
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the ResourceQuota
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuota): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Service Quota Status

Check current resource usage status for Skills service.

# Get Skills ResourceQuota status
kubectl get resourcequota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service -o jsonpath='{.status}'
{"hard":{"limits.cpu":"8","limits.memory":"16Gi","pods":"20","requests.cpu":"4","requests.memory":"8Gi"},"used":{"limits.cpu":"4","limits.memory":"8Gi","pods":"12","requests.cpu":"2","requests.memory":"4Gi"}}
Example 2: Get User Service Storage Status

View storage quota status for User authentication data.

# Get User ResourceQuota status with jq
kubectl get quota user-storage-quota -n user-service -o json | jq '.status'
{ "hard": { "persistentvolumeclaims": "5", "requests.cpu": "3", "requests.memory": "6Gi", "requests.storage": "50Gi" }, "used": { "persistentvolumeclaims": "4", "requests.cpu": "2500m", "requests.memory": "5Gi", "requests.storage": "45Gi" } }
Example 3: Get Author Service Object Count Status

Check how many Kubernetes objects are currently used in Author service.

# Get Author ResourceQuota used resources
kubectl get resourcequota author-object-quota -n author-service -o jsonpath='{.status.used}'
{"configmaps":"6","deployments.apps":"2","pods":"8","secrets":"5","services":"3"}
Example 4: Get Location Service Status in YAML

View Location service quota status in YAML format.

# Get Location ResourceQuota status as YAML
kubectl get quota location-priority-quota -n location-service -o yaml | grep -A 15 "status:"
status: hard: pods: "10" requests.cpu: "5" requests.memory: 10Gi used: pods: "10" requests.cpu: 4500m requests.memory: 9Gi
Example 5: Get JobPost Service Complete Status

View comprehensive status for main JobPost application quota.

# Describe JobPost ResourceQuota to see full status
kubectl describe quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service | grep -A 20 "Resource"
Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- configmaps 12 20 cronjobs.batch 3 10 deployments.apps 5 8 jobs.batch 45 100 limits.cpu 18 24 limits.memory 36Gi 48Gi persistentvolumeclaims 8 12 pods 28 40 requests.cpu 9 12 requests.memory 18Gi 24Gi requests.storage 100Gi 150Gi secrets 10 15 services 6 10 statefulsets.apps 2 4

list - list or watch objects of kind ResourceQuota

List ResourceQuotas in a specific namespace with optional filtering and pagination.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace to list ResourceQuotas from
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): boolean - allow watch bookmarks
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query): integer - maximum number of results
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resource version
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout in seconds
  • watch (in query): boolean - watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuotaList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List Skills Service ResourceQuotas

Get all ResourceQuotas in the Skills namespace.

# List ResourceQuotas in Skills namespace
kubectl get resourcequotas -n skills-service
NAME AGE REQUEST LIMIT skills-compute-quota 5d requests.cpu: 2/4, requests.memory: 4Gi/8Gi limits.cpu: 4/8, limits.memory: 8Gi/16Gi
Example 2: List User Service ResourceQuotas with Labels

Filter User service ResourceQuotas by component label.

# List User ResourceQuotas filtered by label
kubectl get quota -n user-service -l component=storage
NAME AGE REQUEST user-storage-quota 5d persistentvolumeclaims: 4/5, requests.cpu: 2500m/3, requests.memory: 5Gi/6Gi, requests.storage: 45Gi/50Gi
Example 3: List Author Service ResourceQuotas in Wide Format

View Author service ResourceQuotas with additional details.

# List Author ResourceQuotas with wide output
kubectl get resourcequotas -n author-service -o wide
NAME AGE REQUEST LIMIT author-object-quota 5d configmaps: 6/10, deployments.apps: 2/5, pods: 8/15, secrets: 5/10, services: 3/5
Example 4: Watch Location Service ResourceQuotas

Monitor changes to Location service ResourceQuotas in real-time.

# Watch Location ResourceQuotas for changes
kubectl get quota -n location-service --watch
NAME AGE REQUEST location-priority-quota 5d pods: 10/10, requests.cpu: 4500m/5, requests.memory: 9Gi/10Gi location-priority-quota 5d pods: 9/10, requests.cpu: 4m/5, requests.memory: 8Gi/10Gi
Example 5: List JobPost Service ResourceQuotas with Custom Columns

Display JobPost service ResourceQuotas with specific columns.

# List JobPost ResourceQuotas with custom columns
kubectl get resourcequotas -n jobpost-service -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,PODS-USED:.status.used.pods,PODS-HARD:.status.hard.pods
NAME PODS-USED PODS-HARD jobpost-comprehensive-quota 28 40

list - list or watch objects of kind ResourceQuota (all namespaces)

List ResourceQuotas across all namespaces in the cluster.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/resourcequotas
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): boolean - allow watch bookmarks
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query): integer - maximum number of results
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resource version
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout in seconds
  • watch (in query): boolean - watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuotaList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All anihpj Application ResourceQuotas

View all ResourceQuotas across all namespaces for the jobpost application.

# List all ResourceQuotas with app label
kubectl get resourcequotas --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj
NAMESPACE NAME AGE skills-service skills-compute-quota 5d user-service user-storage-quota 5d author-service author-object-quota 5d location-service location-priority-quota 5d jobpost-service jobpost-comprehensive-quota 5d
Example 2: List ResourceQuotas with Specific Label Selector

Filter ResourceQuotas across all namespaces by service type.

# List ResourceQuotas for storage components
kubectl get quota --all-namespaces -l component=storage
NAMESPACE NAME AGE user-service user-storage-quota 5d
Example 3: List All ResourceQuotas with Custom Columns

Display cluster-wide ResourceQuotas with specific information.

# List all ResourceQuotas with namespace and CPU info
kubectl get resourcequotas --all-namespaces -o custom-columns=NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,CPU-USED:.status.used.requests\\.cpu,CPU-HARD:.status.hard.requests\\.cpu
NAMESPACE NAME CPU-USED CPU-HARD skills-service skills-compute-quota 2 4 user-service user-storage-quota 2500m 3 author-service author-object-quota location-service location-priority-quota 4500m 5 jobpost-service jobpost-comprehensive-quota 9 12
Example 4: List ResourceQuotas in JSON Format

Get all ResourceQuotas as JSON for programmatic processing.

# List all ResourceQuotas as JSON and extract names
kubectl get resourcequotas --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {namespace: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'
{ "namespace": "skills-service", "name": "skills-compute-quota" } { "namespace": "user-service", "name": "user-storage-quota" } { "namespace": "author-service", "name": "author-object-quota" } { "namespace": "location-service", "name": "location-priority-quota" } { "namespace": "jobpost-service", "name": "jobpost-comprehensive-quota" }
Example 5: Count Total ResourceQuotas Across Cluster

Count how many ResourceQuotas exist in the entire anihpj application cluster.

# Count all ResourceQuotas
kubectl get resourcequotas --all-namespaces --no-headers | wc -l
5

create - create a ResourceQuota

Create a new ResourceQuota in a namespace to enforce resource limits.

HTTP Request
POST /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace to create the ResourceQuota in
  • body: ResourceQuota, required - ResourceQuota object to create
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager creating this resource
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuota): OK
  • 201 (ResourceQuota): Created
  • 202 (ResourceQuota): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Basic Skills Service ResourceQuota

Create compute quota for Skills microservice namespace.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: skills-compute-quota
  namespace: skills-service
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "20"
# Create Skills ResourceQuota
kubectl apply -f skills-quota.yaml
resourcequota/skills-compute-quota created
Example 2: Create User Service Storage ResourceQuota

Set up storage limits for User authentication data.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: user-storage-quota
  namespace: user-service
spec:
  hard:
    requests.storage: 50Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "5"
    requests.cpu: "3"
    requests.memory: 6Gi
# Create User ResourceQuota
kubectl create -f user-storage-quota.yaml
resourcequota/user-storage-quota created
Example 3: Create Author Service ResourceQuota with Dry Run

Test ResourceQuota creation for Author service without applying.

# Create Author ResourceQuota with dry-run
kubectl create quota author-object-quota --hard=pods=15,services=5,configmaps=10 -n author-service --dry-run=server -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: ResourceQuota metadata: creationTimestamp: null name: author-object-quota namespace: author-service spec: hard: configmaps: "10" pods: "15" services: "5" status: {}
Example 4: Create Location Service ResourceQuota Imperatively

Quickly create ResourceQuota for Location service using kubectl create.

# Create Location ResourceQuota imperatively
kubectl create quota location-priority-quota --hard=requests.cpu=5,requests.memory=10Gi,pods=10 -n location-service
resourcequota/location-priority-quota created
Example 5: Create Comprehensive JobPost Service ResourceQuota

Create complete ResourceQuota covering all resource types for JobPost application.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: jobpost-comprehensive-quota
  namespace: jobpost-service
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "12"
    requests.memory: 24Gi
    limits.cpu: "24"
    limits.memory: 48Gi
    requests.storage: 150Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "12"
    pods: "40"
    services: "10"
    configmaps: "20"
    secrets: "15"
    deployments.apps: "8"
    statefulsets.apps: "4"
    jobs.batch: "100"
    cronjobs.batch: "10"
# Create JobPost comprehensive ResourceQuota
kubectl apply -f jobpost-comprehensive-quota.yaml
resourcequota/jobpost-comprehensive-quota created

update - replace the specified ResourceQuota

Replace an existing ResourceQuota with a new definition.

HTTP Request
PUT /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the ResourceQuota
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the ResourceQuota
  • body: ResourceQuota, required - updated ResourceQuota object
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager updating this resource
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuota): OK
  • 201 (ResourceQuota): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Service CPU Limits

Increase CPU limits for Skills service quota.

# Edit and update Skills ResourceQuota
kubectl edit resourcequota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service
# Change requests.cpu from "4" to "6" and limits.cpu from "8" to "12"
resourcequota/skills-compute-quota edited
Example 2: Update User Service Storage Quota

Increase storage allocation for User authentication data.

# Get current quota, modify, and apply
kubectl get quota user-storage-quota -n user-service -o yaml > user-quota.yaml
# Edit user-quota.yaml: change requests.storage from 50Gi to 100Gi
kubectl apply -f user-quota.yaml
resourcequota/user-storage-quota configured
Example 3: Update Author Service Object Counts

Modify object count limits for Author content management.

# Update Author ResourceQuota with kubectl replace
kubectl get quota author-object-quota -n author-service -o yaml | \
  sed 's/pods: "15"/pods: "20"/' | \
  kubectl replace -f -
resourcequota/author-object-quota replaced
Example 4: Update Location Service with Dry Run

Test ResourceQuota update for Location service before applying.

# Update Location ResourceQuota with dry-run
kubectl apply -f location-quota-updated.yaml --dry-run=server
resourcequota/location-priority-quota configured (server dry run)
Example 5: Update JobPost Service Comprehensive Quota

Update multiple resource limits for JobPost application.

# Update JobPost ResourceQuota
kubectl apply -f jobpost-comprehensive-quota-v2.yaml
resourcequota/jobpost-comprehensive-quota configured

update - replace status of the specified ResourceQuota

Update the status subresource of a ResourceQuota (typically done by the system).

HTTP Request
PUT /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the ResourceQuota
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the ResourceQuota
  • body: ResourceQuota, required - updated ResourceQuota object
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager updating this resource
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuota): OK
  • 201 (ResourceQuota): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: View Skills Service Status Update

Monitor automatic status updates for Skills service quota.

# Watch Skills ResourceQuota status changes
kubectl get quota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service --watch -o jsonpath='{.status.used}'
{"limits.cpu":"4","limits.memory":"8Gi","pods":"12","requests.cpu":"2","requests.memory":"4Gi"} {"limits.cpu":"5","limits.memory":"10Gi","pods":"13","requests.cpu":"2500m","requests.memory":"5Gi"}
Example 2: Check User Service Status After Deployment

Verify User service quota status updates after creating new resources.

# Check User ResourceQuota status before and after
kubectl describe quota user-storage-quota -n user-service
Name: user-storage-quota Namespace: user-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- persistentvolumeclaims 4 5 requests.cpu 2500m 3 requests.memory 5Gi 6Gi requests.storage 45Gi 50Gi
Example 3: Monitor Author Service Status in Real-time

Track Author service quota status changes as objects are created/deleted.

# Monitor Author ResourceQuota status updates
kubectl get resourcequota author-object-quota -n author-service -w
NAME AGE REQUEST author-object-quota 5d configmaps: 6/10, deployments.apps: 2/5, pods: 8/15, secrets: 5/10, services: 3/5 author-object-quota 5d configmaps: 7/10, deployments.apps: 2/5, pods: 9/15, secrets: 5/10, services: 3/5
Example 4: View Location Service Status Subresource

Access the status subresource directly for Location service quota.

# Get Location ResourceQuota status subresource
kubectl get quota location-priority-quota -n location-service -o yaml | grep -A 10 "status:"
status: hard: pods: "10" requests.cpu: "5" requests.memory: 10Gi used: pods: "9" requests.cpu: 4m requests.memory: 8Gi
Example 5: Track JobPost Service Status Over Time

Monitor JobPost service quota status to track resource consumption trends.

# Continuously check JobPost ResourceQuota status
watch kubectl describe quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service
Every 2.0s: kubectl describe quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service Name: jobpost-comprehensive-quota Namespace: jobpost-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- configmaps 12 20 cronjobs.batch 3 10 deployments.apps 5 8 jobs.batch 45 100 pods 28 40

patch - partially update the specified ResourceQuota

Partially update a ResourceQuota using strategic merge patch, JSON merge patch, or JSON patch.

HTTP Request
PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the ResourceQuota
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the ResourceQuota
  • body: Patch, required - patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager patching this resource
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • force (in query): boolean - force apply the patch
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuota): OK
  • 201 (ResourceQuota): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Service CPU Limit

Update only the CPU request limit for Skills service using JSON patch.

# Patch Skills ResourceQuota CPU limit
kubectl patch resourcequota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service --type='json' -p='[{"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/hard/requests.cpu", "value": "6"}]'
resourcequota/skills-compute-quota patched
Example 2: Patch User Service Memory with Merge Patch

Update memory limits for User service using merge patch.

# Patch User ResourceQuota memory
kubectl patch quota user-storage-quota -n user-service --type=merge -p='{"spec":{"hard":{"requests.memory":"10Gi"}}}'
resourcequota/user-storage-quota patched
Example 3: Patch Author Service Labels

Add or update labels on Author service ResourceQuota using strategic merge.

# Patch Author ResourceQuota labels
kubectl patch resourcequota author-object-quota -n author-service -p='{"metadata":{"labels":{"environment":"production","tier":"backend"}}}'
resourcequota/author-object-quota patched
Example 4: Patch Location Service Pod Limit with JSON Patch

Increase pod limit for Location service using JSON patch operations.

# Patch Location ResourceQuota pod limit
kubectl patch quota location-priority-quota -n location-service --type='json' -p='[{"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/hard/pods", "value": "15"}]'
resourcequota/location-priority-quota patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost Service Multiple Limits

Update multiple resource limits for JobPost service in one patch operation.

# Patch JobPost ResourceQuota multiple limits
kubectl patch resourcequota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service --type=merge -p='{"spec":{"hard":{"pods":"50","requests.cpu":"15","requests.memory":"30Gi"}}}'
resourcequota/jobpost-comprehensive-quota patched

patch - partially update status of the specified ResourceQuota

Partially update the status subresource of a ResourceQuota (typically done by system controllers).

HTTP Request
PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the ResourceQuota
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the ResourceQuota
  • body: Patch, required - patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager patching this resource
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • force (in query): boolean - force apply the patch
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuota): OK
  • 201 (ResourceQuota): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Monitor Skills Service Status Patches

Observe automatic status patches as Skills service resources change.

# Watch Skills ResourceQuota for status updates
kubectl get quota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service -o yaml -w | grep -A 5 "status:"
status: hard: limits.cpu: "8" limits.memory: 16Gi pods: "20" used: limits.cpu: "4" limits.memory: 8Gi pods: "12"
Example 2: Track User Service Status Changes

Monitor how User service quota status updates when PVCs are created.

# Watch User ResourceQuota status field
kubectl get resourcequota user-storage-quota -n user-service --watch-only -o jsonpath='{.status}'
{"hard":{"persistentvolumeclaims":"5","requests.cpu":"3","requests.memory":"6Gi","requests.storage":"50Gi"},"used":{"persistentvolumeclaims":"4","requests.cpu":"2500m","requests.memory":"5Gi","requests.storage":"45Gi"}}
Example 3: View Author Service Status Patch Events

Check events related to Author service quota status updates.

# Get events related to Author ResourceQuota
kubectl get events -n author-service --field-selector involvedObject.name=author-object-quota
LAST SEEN TYPE REASON OBJECT MESSAGE 2m Normal SuccessfulCreate resourcequota/author-object-quota Created ResourceQuota 1m Normal QuotaUpdated resourcequota/author-object-quota Updated quota status
Example 4: Monitor Location Service Status After Scale

Watch Location service quota status update when scaling deployments.

# Describe Location ResourceQuota to see status
kubectl describe quota location-priority-quota -n location-service
Name: location-priority-quota Namespace: location-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- pods 10 10 requests.cpu 5 5 requests.memory 10Gi 10Gi
Example 5: Track JobPost Service Status Patch History

View JobPost service quota status changes over time using kubectl diff.

# Compare JobPost ResourceQuota status
kubectl get quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: ResourceQuota metadata: name: jobpost-comprehensive-quota namespace: jobpost-service spec: hard: pods: "40" requests.cpu: "12" status: hard: pods: "40" requests.cpu: "12" used: pods: "28" requests.cpu: "9"

delete - delete a ResourceQuota

Delete a specific ResourceQuota from a namespace, removing resource restrictions.

HTTP Request
DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the ResourceQuota
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the ResourceQuota
  • body: DeleteOptions - deletion options
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - grace period in seconds
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - deletion propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200 (ResourceQuota): OK
  • 202 (ResourceQuota): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Service ResourceQuota

Remove resource quota restrictions from Skills service namespace.

# Delete Skills ResourceQuota
kubectl delete resourcequota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service
resourcequota "skills-compute-quota" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Service ResourceQuota with Confirmation

Delete User service quota after verifying no critical resources depend on it.

# Verify and delete User ResourceQuota
kubectl get quota user-storage-quota -n user-service
kubectl delete quota user-storage-quota -n user-service
NAME AGE REQUEST user-storage-quota 5d persistentvolumeclaims: 4/5, requests.cpu: 2500m/3, requests.memory: 5Gi/6Gi resourcequota "user-storage-quota" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author Service ResourceQuota with Dry Run

Test deletion of Author service quota without actually removing it.

# Dry run delete Author ResourceQuota
kubectl delete resourcequota author-object-quota -n author-service --dry-run=server
resourcequota "author-object-quota" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete Location Service ResourceQuota from YAML

Delete Location service quota using the original YAML definition.

# Delete Location ResourceQuota from file
kubectl delete -f location-quota.yaml
resourcequota "location-priority-quota" deleted
Example 5: Force Delete JobPost Service ResourceQuota

Forcefully remove JobPost service quota immediately.

# Force delete JobPost ResourceQuota
kubectl delete quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service --grace-period=0 --force
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. The resource may continue to run on the cluster indefinitely. resourcequota "jobpost-comprehensive-quota" force deleted

deletecollection - delete collection of ResourceQuota

Delete multiple ResourceQuotas in a namespace matching specified criteria.

HTTP Request
DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace to delete ResourceQuotas from
  • body: DeleteOptions - deletion options
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector to filter resources
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - grace period in seconds
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector to filter resources
  • limit (in query): integer - maximum number of resources to delete
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - deletion propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resource version
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout in seconds
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All ResourceQuotas in Skills Namespace

Remove all quota restrictions from Skills service namespace.

# Delete all ResourceQuotas in Skills namespace
kubectl delete resourcequotas --all -n skills-service
resourcequota "skills-compute-quota" deleted resourcequota "skills-storage-quota" deleted
Example 2: Delete ResourceQuotas by Label Selector in User Service

Delete User service ResourceQuotas matching specific component label.

# Delete User ResourceQuotas with component=storage label
kubectl delete quota -l component=storage -n user-service
resourcequota "user-storage-quota" deleted
Example 3: Delete ResourceQuotas with Dry Run in Author Service

Preview which Author service ResourceQuotas would be deleted.

# Dry run delete all ResourceQuotas in Author namespace
kubectl delete resourcequotas --all -n author-service --dry-run=server
resourcequota "author-object-quota" deleted (server dry run) resourcequota "author-compute-quota" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete ResourceQuotas Matching Multiple Labels in Location Service

Delete Location service ResourceQuotas with specific label combinations.

# Delete Location ResourceQuotas matching app and tier labels
kubectl delete quota -l app=anihpj,tier=production -n location-service
resourcequota "location-priority-quota" deleted resourcequota "location-compute-quota" deleted
Example 5: Delete All anihpj Application ResourceQuotas

Remove all ResourceQuotas from all anihpj jobpost application namespaces.

# Delete ResourceQuotas from all anihpj namespaces
for ns in skills-service user-service author-service location-service jobpost-service; do
  kubectl delete resourcequotas --all -n $ns
done
resourcequota "skills-compute-quota" deleted resourcequota "user-storage-quota" deleted resourcequota "author-object-quota" deleted resourcequota "location-priority-quota" deleted resourcequota "jobpost-comprehensive-quota" deleted

NetworkPolicy

NetworkPolicy Overview

NetworkPolicySpec - Specification for network traffic rules
NetworkPolicyList - Collection of NetworkPolicies
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: NetworkPolicy v1 API | API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1

Full Description

NetworkPolicy describes what network traffic is allowed for a set of Pods in the anihpj jobpost application. It provides fine-grained control over ingress and egress network traffic to Pods based on labels, namespaces, IP blocks, and ports. NetworkPolicies are essential for implementing zero-trust security models where Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services can communicate only through explicitly allowed paths.

In the anihpj application, NetworkPolicies ensure that each microservice (Skills management, User authentication, Author content, Location data, and JobPost operations) operates in isolation with controlled network access. This prevents unauthorized service-to-service communication and limits the attack surface if any service is compromised. NetworkPolicies use label selectors to target Pods and define both ingress (incoming) and egress (outgoing) traffic rules.

Syntax

# Create NetworkPolicy from YAML
kubectl apply -f networkpolicy.yaml

# Get NetworkPolicy
kubectl get networkpolicy skills-network-policy -n skills-service

# Describe NetworkPolicy to see rules
kubectl describe networkpolicy user-network-policy -n user-service

# List all NetworkPolicies in namespace
kubectl get networkpolicies -n author-service

# Delete NetworkPolicy
kubectl delete networkpolicy location-network-policy -n location-service

# Get NetworkPolicy in YAML format
kubectl get networkpolicy jobpost-network-policy -n jobpost-service -o yaml

# List all NetworkPolicies across namespaces
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces

YAML Structure

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: jobpost-api-policy
  namespace: jobpost-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: network-security
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-api
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: frontend
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: user-service
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres-db
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector: {}
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version (networking.k8s.io/v1)
  • kind: string - Resource type (NetworkPolicy)
  • metadata (ObjectMeta) - Standard object metadata
    • name: string - NetworkPolicy name
    • namespace: string - Namespace where policy applies
    • labels: map[string]string - Labels for organization
  • spec (NetworkPolicySpec) - Specification of desired network behavior
    • podSelector (LabelSelector), required - Selects Pods to which policy applies; empty selector matches all Pods in namespace
    • policyTypes: []string - Types of rules ["Ingress"], ["Egress"], or ["Ingress", "Egress"]
    • ingress: []NetworkPolicyIngressRule - List of ingress rules
      • from: []NetworkPolicyPeer - Sources allowed to access selected Pods
      • ports: []NetworkPolicyPort - Ports on which traffic is allowed
    • egress: []NetworkPolicyEgressRule - List of egress rules
      • to: []NetworkPolicyPeer - Destinations for outgoing traffic
      • ports: []NetworkPolicyPort - Destination ports for outgoing traffic

Return Value

  • 200 (NetworkPolicy): OK - Returns the NetworkPolicy object with current state
  • 201 (NetworkPolicy): Created - NetworkPolicy successfully created
  • 202 (NetworkPolicy): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Service Basic Ingress Policy

Allow only frontend pods to access Skills API on port 8000.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: skills-ingress-policy
  namespace: skills-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: skills
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: skills-api
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: frontend
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
# Apply Skills NetworkPolicy
kubectl apply -f skills-ingress-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/skills-ingress-policy created
Example 2: User Service Database Egress Policy

Allow User service to access only PostgreSQL database on port 5432.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: user-db-egress-policy
  namespace: user-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: user
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: user-api
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres-db
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector: {}
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
# Create User service egress policy
kubectl apply -f user-db-egress-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/user-db-egress-policy created
Example 3: Author Service Namespace Isolation

Allow Author service to receive traffic only from User and JobPost namespaces.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: author-namespace-policy
  namespace: author-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: author
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: author-api
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: user-service
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: jobpost-service
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
# Apply Author namespace isolation
kubectl apply -f author-namespace-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/author-namespace-policy created
Example 4: Location Service IP Block Policy

Allow Location service to receive traffic from specific IP ranges.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: location-ipblock-policy
  namespace: location-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: location
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: location-api
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - ipBlock:
            cidr: 10.0.0.0/16
            except:
              - 10.0.5.0/24
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
# Create Location IP block policy
kubectl apply -f location-ipblock-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/location-ipblock-policy created
Example 5: JobPost Service Comprehensive Policy

Complete ingress and egress policy for JobPost service with multiple rules.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: jobpost-comprehensive-policy
  namespace: jobpost-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: jobpost
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-api
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: frontend
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: user-service
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres-db
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: skills-service
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: author-service
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: location-service
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector: {}
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
# Apply comprehensive JobPost policy
kubectl apply -f jobpost-comprehensive-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/jobpost-comprehensive-policy created

NetworkPolicySpec

Full Description

NetworkPolicySpec defines the specification of a NetworkPolicy, describing the network traffic rules for selected Pods in the anihpj jobpost application. The spec contains a podSelector to identify target Pods, policyTypes to indicate whether ingress and/or egress rules apply, and the actual ingress and egress rule lists. An empty podSelector matches all Pods in the namespace, while specific label selectors target subsets like Skills API pods, User authentication pods, or JobPost processing pods.

The NetworkPolicySpec structure allows for complex traffic control scenarios. Ingress rules define what traffic can reach the selected Pods (sources and ports), while egress rules control what traffic can leave those Pods (destinations and ports). Each rule can combine pod selectors, namespace selectors, and IP blocks for fine-grained control. This is essential for implementing micro-segmentation in the anihpj application where Services need controlled communication paths.

Syntax
# Get NetworkPolicySpec using jsonpath
kubectl get networkpolicy skills-network-policy -n skills-service -o jsonpath='{.spec}'

# View podSelector
kubectl get networkpolicy user-network-policy -n user-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.podSelector}'

# Check policyTypes
kubectl get networkpolicy author-network-policy -n author-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.policyTypes}'
Parameters Description
  • podSelector (LabelSelector), required - Selects Pods to which this NetworkPolicy applies
    • Empty selector matches all Pods in namespace
    • Specific matchLabels targets pods like app=jobpost-api
  • policyTypes: []string - Rule types: ["Ingress"], ["Egress"], or ["Ingress", "Egress"]
    • If not specified, defaults based on presence of ingress/egress sections
    • Must explicitly specify ["Egress"] for egress-only policies
  • ingress: []NetworkPolicyIngressRule - Ingress rules for incoming traffic
    • from: []NetworkPolicyPeer - Sources allowed to access Pods
    • ports: []NetworkPolicyPort - Ports on which traffic is allowed
  • egress: []NetworkPolicyEgressRule - Egress rules for outgoing traffic
    • to: []NetworkPolicyPeer - Destinations for outgoing traffic
    • ports: []NetworkPolicyPort - Destination ports
Return Value

Returns a NetworkPolicySpec object containing podSelector, policyTypes, and ingress/egress rule arrays.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Skills Service Spec with Pod Selector

NetworkPolicySpec targeting Skills API pods for ingress control.

# View Skills NetworkPolicy spec
kubectl get networkpolicy skills-ingress-policy -n skills-service -o yaml | grep -A 20 "spec:"
spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: skills-api policyTypes: - Ingress ingress: - from: - podSelector: matchLabels: app: frontend ports: - protocol: TCP port: 8000
Example 2: User Service Egress Spec

NetworkPolicySpec with egress rules for User database access.

# Extract User service egress spec
kubectl get networkpolicy user-db-egress-policy -n user-service -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq .
{ "podSelector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "user-api" } }, "policyTypes": [ "Egress" ], "egress": [ { "to": [ { "podSelector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "postgres-db" } } } ], "ports": [ { "protocol": "TCP", "port": 5432 } ] } ] }
Example 3: Author Service Namespace Selector Spec

NetworkPolicySpec using namespace selectors for Author service isolation.

# View Author service spec with namespace selection
kubectl describe networkpolicy author-namespace-policy -n author-service | grep -A 15 "Spec:"
Spec: PodSelector: app=author-api Allowing ingress traffic: To Port: 8000/TCP From: NamespaceSelector: name=user-service NamespaceSelector: name=jobpost-service Policy Types: Ingress
Example 4: Location Service IP Block Spec

NetworkPolicySpec with IP block configuration for Location service.

# Get Location service IP block spec
kubectl get networkpolicy location-ipblock-policy -n location-service -o yaml | grep -A 12 "spec:"
spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: location-api policyTypes: - Ingress ingress: - from: - ipBlock: cidr: 10.0.0.0/16 except: - 10.0.5.0/24 ports: - protocol: TCP port: 8000
Example 5: JobPost Service Multi-Rule Spec

Complex NetworkPolicySpec with both ingress and egress for JobPost service.

# View comprehensive JobPost spec
kubectl get networkpolicy jobpost-comprehensive-policy -n jobpost-service -o json | jq '.spec'
{ "podSelector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "jobpost-api" } }, "policyTypes": [ "Ingress", "Egress" ], "ingress": [ { "from": [ { "podSelector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "frontend" } } }, { "namespaceSelector": { "matchLabels": { "name": "user-service" } } } ], "ports": [ { "protocol": "TCP", "port": 8000 } ] } ], "egress": [ { "to": [ { "podSelector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "postgres-db" } } } ], "ports": [ { "protocol": "TCP", "port": 5432 } ] } ] }

NetworkPolicyList

Full Description

NetworkPolicyList is a collection of NetworkPolicy objects returned by list operations in the anihpj jobpost application. It contains metadata about the list itself (like resource version for consistency) and an array of NetworkPolicy items. This structure is useful for auditing all network policies across namespaces, verifying security configurations, and ensuring that Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services have appropriate network isolation policies in place.

When listing NetworkPolicies, administrators can filter by namespace, labels, or field selectors to find specific policies. The list provides a comprehensive view of the network security posture, showing which Pods are protected by policies and what traffic rules are enforced. This is critical for compliance auditing and security reviews in production environments where the anihpj application handles sensitive job posting data.

Syntax
# List NetworkPolicies in specific namespace
kubectl get networkpolicies -n skills-service

# List all NetworkPolicies across all namespaces
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces

# List with custom columns
kubectl get networkpolicies -n user-service -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,POD-SELECTOR:.spec.podSelector
Parameters Description
  • apiVersion: string - API version (networking.k8s.io/v1)
  • kind: string - Resource type (NetworkPolicyList)
  • metadata (ListMeta) - Standard list metadata
    • resourceVersion: string - Version for consistency
    • continue: string - Token for pagination
  • items: []NetworkPolicy, required - Array of NetworkPolicy objects
Return Value

Returns a NetworkPolicyList object containing metadata and an array of NetworkPolicy items matching the query criteria.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List Skills Service NetworkPolicies

Get all NetworkPolicies protecting Skills microservice.

# List NetworkPolicies in Skills namespace
kubectl get networkpolicies -n skills-service
NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE skills-ingress-policy app=skills-api 5d
Example 2: List User Service Policies with Labels

Filter User service NetworkPolicies by application label.

# List User NetworkPolicies with app label
kubectl get networkpolicies -n user-service -l app=anihpj
NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE user-db-egress-policy app=user-api 5d
Example 3: List All anihpj NetworkPolicies

View NetworkPolicies across all anihpj application namespaces.

# List all NetworkPolicies with namespace column
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj
NAMESPACE NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE skills-service skills-ingress-policy app=skills-api 5d user-service user-db-egress-policy app=user-api 5d author-service author-namespace-policy app=author-api 5d location-service location-ipblock-policy app=location-api 5d jobpost-service jobpost-comprehensive-policy app=jobpost-api 5d
Example 4: List NetworkPolicies in JSON Format

Get Location service NetworkPolicies as JSON for programmatic processing.

# Get NetworkPolicies as JSON
kubectl get networkpolicies -n location-service -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, podSelector: .spec.podSelector}'
{ "name": "location-ipblock-policy", "podSelector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "location-api" } } }
Example 5: Count Total NetworkPolicies

Count how many NetworkPolicies protect the JobPost application cluster.

# Count all NetworkPolicies
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj --no-headers | wc -l
5

Operations

The following operations are available for managing NetworkPolicy resources in the anihpj jobpost application.

get - Read the specified NetworkPolicy
list - List NetworkPolicies in namespace
list (all namespaces) - List NetworkPolicies cluster-wide
create - Create a NetworkPolicy
update - Replace the specified NetworkPolicy
patch - Partially update NetworkPolicy
delete - Delete a NetworkPolicy
deletecollection - Delete multiple NetworkPolicies

get - read the specified NetworkPolicy

Retrieve detailed information about a specific NetworkPolicy in a namespace.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the NetworkPolicy
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the NetworkPolicy
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (NetworkPolicy): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Service NetworkPolicy

Retrieve Skills service network policy in YAML format.

# Get Skills NetworkPolicy
kubectl get networkpolicy skills-ingress-policy -n skills-service -o yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: skills-ingress-policy namespace: skills-service spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: skills-api policyTypes: - Ingress ingress: - from: - podSelector: matchLabels: app: frontend ports: - protocol: TCP port: 8000
Example 2: Get User Service Policy with Describe

View User service network policy with detailed description.

# Describe User NetworkPolicy
kubectl describe networkpolicy user-db-egress-policy -n user-service
Name: user-db-egress-policy Namespace: user-service Labels: app=anihpj service=user Spec: PodSelector: app=user-api Allowing egress traffic: To Port: 5432/TCP To: PodSelector: app=postgres-db To Port: 53/TCP To Port: 53/UDP To: NamespaceSelector: Policy Types: Egress
Example 3: Get Author Service Policy in JSON

Retrieve Author service network policy as JSON.

# Get Author NetworkPolicy in JSON
kubectl get networkpolicy author-namespace-policy -n author-service -o json
{ "apiVersion": "networking.k8s.io/v1", "kind": "NetworkPolicy", "metadata": { "name": "author-namespace-policy", "namespace": "author-service" }, "spec": { "podSelector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "author-api" } }, "policyTypes": ["Ingress"], "ingress": [{ "from": [ {"namespaceSelector": {"matchLabels": {"name": "user-service"}}}, {"namespaceSelector": {"matchLabels": {"name": "jobpost-service"}}} ], "ports": [{"protocol": "TCP", "port": 8000}] }] } }
Example 4: Get Location Service Pod Selector

Extract only the pod selector from Location service policy.

# Get Location NetworkPolicy pod selector
kubectl get networkpolicy location-ipblock-policy -n location-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.podSelector}'
{"matchLabels":{"app":"location-api"}}
Example 5: Get JobPost Service Policy Rules

View JobPost service comprehensive network policy rules.

# Get JobPost NetworkPolicy with formatting
kubectl get networkpolicy jobpost-comprehensive-policy -n jobpost-service -o yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: jobpost-comprehensive-policy namespace: jobpost-service spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: jobpost-api policyTypes: - Ingress - Egress ingress: - from: - podSelector: matchLabels: app: frontend - namespaceSelector: matchLabels: name: user-service ports: - protocol: TCP port: 8000 egress: - to: - podSelector: matchLabels: app: postgres-db ports: - protocol: TCP port: 5432

list - list or watch objects of kind NetworkPolicy

List NetworkPolicies in a specific namespace with optional filtering.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace to list NetworkPolicies from
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): boolean - allow watch bookmarks
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query): integer - maximum number of results
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resource version
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout in seconds
  • watch (in query): boolean - watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (NetworkPolicyList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List Skills Service NetworkPolicies

Get all network policies in Skills namespace.

# List Skills NetworkPolicies
kubectl get networkpolicies -n skills-service
NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE skills-ingress-policy app=skills-api 5d
Example 2: List User Service Policies with Labels

Filter User service NetworkPolicies by app label.

# List User NetworkPolicies with label selector
kubectl get networkpolicies -n user-service -l app=anihpj
NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE user-db-egress-policy app=user-api 5d
Example 3: List Author Service Policies in Wide Format

View Author service NetworkPolicies with additional details.

# List Author NetworkPolicies with wide output
kubectl get networkpolicies -n author-service -o wide
NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE author-namespace-policy app=author-api 5d
Example 4: Watch Location Service NetworkPolicies

Monitor changes to Location service NetworkPolicies in real-time.

# Watch Location NetworkPolicies
kubectl get networkpolicies -n location-service --watch
NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE location-ipblock-policy app=location-api 5d
Example 5: List JobPost Service Policies with Custom Columns

Display JobPost NetworkPolicies with specific column format.

# List JobPost NetworkPolicies with custom columns
kubectl get networkpolicies -n jobpost-service -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,POD-SELECTOR:.spec.podSelector.matchLabels
NAME POD-SELECTOR jobpost-comprehensive-policy map[app:jobpost-api]

list - list or watch objects of kind NetworkPolicy (all namespaces)

List NetworkPolicies across all namespaces in the cluster.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/networkpolicies
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): boolean - allow watch bookmarks
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query): integer - maximum number of results
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resource version
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout in seconds
  • watch (in query): boolean - watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (NetworkPolicyList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All anihpj NetworkPolicies

View all NetworkPolicies across anihpj application namespaces.

# List all NetworkPolicies with app label
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj
NAMESPACE NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE skills-service skills-ingress-policy app=skills-api 5d user-service user-db-egress-policy app=user-api 5d author-service author-namespace-policy app=author-api 5d location-service location-ipblock-policy app=location-api 5d jobpost-service jobpost-comprehensive-policy app=jobpost-api 5d
Example 2: List Policies with Service Label

Filter NetworkPolicies by service component label.

# List NetworkPolicies for specific service type
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces -l service=user
NAMESPACE NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE user-service user-db-egress-policy app=user-api 5d
Example 3: List All Policies with Custom Columns

Display cluster-wide NetworkPolicies with specific information.

# List all NetworkPolicies with namespace and pod selector
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces -o custom-columns=NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,POD-SELECTOR:.spec.podSelector.matchLabels.app
NAMESPACE NAME POD-SELECTOR skills-service skills-ingress-policy skills-api user-service user-db-egress-policy user-api author-service author-namespace-policy author-api location-service location-ipblock-policy location-api jobpost-service jobpost-comprehensive-policy jobpost-api
Example 4: List Policies in JSON Format

Get all NetworkPolicies as JSON for programmatic processing.

# List all NetworkPolicies as JSON
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {namespace: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'
{ "namespace": "skills-service", "name": "skills-ingress-policy" } { "namespace": "user-service", "name": "user-db-egress-policy" } { "namespace": "author-service", "name": "author-namespace-policy" } { "namespace": "location-service", "name": "location-ipblock-policy" } { "namespace": "jobpost-service", "name": "jobpost-comprehensive-policy" }
Example 5: Count Total NetworkPolicies

Count how many NetworkPolicies exist in the anihpj cluster.

# Count all NetworkPolicies
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces --no-headers | wc -l
5

create - create a NetworkPolicy

Create a new NetworkPolicy in a namespace to control network traffic.

HTTP Request
POST /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace to create the NetworkPolicy in
  • body: NetworkPolicy, required - NetworkPolicy object to create
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager creating this resource
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (NetworkPolicy): OK
  • 201 (NetworkPolicy): Created
  • 202 (NetworkPolicy): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills Service Ingress Policy

Create network policy allowing only frontend to access Skills API.

# Create Skills NetworkPolicy from YAML
kubectl apply -f skills-ingress-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/skills-ingress-policy created
Example 2: Create User Service Egress Policy

Set up egress rules for User service database access.

# Create User NetworkPolicy
kubectl create -f user-db-egress-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/user-db-egress-policy created
Example 3: Create Author Service Policy with Dry Run

Test NetworkPolicy creation for Author service without applying.

# Create Author NetworkPolicy with dry-run
kubectl apply -f author-namespace-policy.yaml --dry-run=server
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/author-namespace-policy created (server dry run)
Example 4: Create Location Service IP Block Policy

Create NetworkPolicy with IP CIDR restrictions for Location service.

# Apply Location NetworkPolicy
kubectl apply -f location-ipblock-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/location-ipblock-policy created
Example 5: Create Comprehensive JobPost Policy

Create complete ingress and egress policy for JobPost service.

# Create JobPost comprehensive NetworkPolicy
kubectl apply -f jobpost-comprehensive-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/jobpost-comprehensive-policy created

update - replace the specified NetworkPolicy

Replace an existing NetworkPolicy with a new definition.

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the NetworkPolicy
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the NetworkPolicy
  • body: NetworkPolicy, required - updated NetworkPolicy object
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager updating this resource
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (NetworkPolicy): OK
  • 201 (NetworkPolicy): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Service Port Rules

Update Skills NetworkPolicy to add additional port.

# Edit and update Skills NetworkPolicy
kubectl edit networkpolicy skills-ingress-policy -n skills-service
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/skills-ingress-policy edited
Example 2: Update User Service Egress Rules

Modify User service NetworkPolicy egress destinations.

# Get, modify, and apply User NetworkPolicy
kubectl get networkpolicy user-db-egress-policy -n user-service -o yaml > user-policy.yaml
# Edit user-policy.yaml
kubectl apply -f user-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/user-db-egress-policy configured
Example 3: Update Author Service Namespace Selectors

Update Author NetworkPolicy to allow additional namespace.

# Update Author NetworkPolicy with kubectl replace
kubectl replace -f author-namespace-policy-updated.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/author-namespace-policy replaced
Example 4: Update Location Service IP Ranges

Modify IP CIDR blocks for Location service policy.

# Update Location NetworkPolicy with dry-run
kubectl apply -f location-ipblock-policy-v2.yaml --dry-run=server
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/location-ipblock-policy configured (server dry run)
Example 5: Update JobPost Service Complete Policy

Update comprehensive JobPost NetworkPolicy with new rules.

# Apply updated JobPost NetworkPolicy
kubectl apply -f jobpost-comprehensive-policy-v2.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/jobpost-comprehensive-policy configured

patch - partially update the specified NetworkPolicy

Partially update a NetworkPolicy using strategic merge patch, JSON merge patch, or JSON patch.

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the NetworkPolicy
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the NetworkPolicy
  • body: Patch, required - patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager patching this resource
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • force (in query): boolean - force apply the patch
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (NetworkPolicy): OK
  • 201 (NetworkPolicy): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Service Port

Add additional port to Skills NetworkPolicy using JSON patch.

# Patch Skills NetworkPolicy to add port 8080
kubectl patch networkpolicy skills-ingress-policy -n skills-service --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/ingress/0/ports/-", "value": {"protocol": "TCP", "port": 8080}}]'
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/skills-ingress-policy patched
Example 2: Patch User Service Labels

Update labels on User NetworkPolicy using merge patch.

# Patch User NetworkPolicy labels
kubectl patch networkpolicy user-db-egress-policy -n user-service --type=merge -p='{"metadata":{"labels":{"environment":"production"}}}'
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/user-db-egress-policy patched
Example 3: Patch Author Service Namespace Selector

Add namespace selector to Author NetworkPolicy.

# Patch Author NetworkPolicy with new namespace
kubectl patch networkpolicy author-namespace-policy -n author-service --type=merge -p='{"spec":{"ingress":[{"from":[{"namespaceSelector":{"matchLabels":{"name":"skills-service"}}}]}]}}'
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/author-namespace-policy patched
Example 4: Patch Location Service IP Exception

Add IP exception to Location NetworkPolicy CIDR block.

# Patch Location NetworkPolicy IP block
kubectl patch networkpolicy location-ipblock-policy -n location-service --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/ingress/0/from/0/ipBlock/except/-", "value": "10.0.6.0/24"}]'
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/location-ipblock-policy patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost Service Egress

Add egress rule to JobPost NetworkPolicy.

# Patch JobPost NetworkPolicy egress rules
kubectl patch networkpolicy jobpost-comprehensive-policy -n jobpost-service --type=merge -p='{"spec":{"egress":[{"to":[{"namespaceSelector":{"matchLabels":{"name":"redis-service"}}}],"ports":[{"protocol":"TCP","port":6379}]}]}}'
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/jobpost-comprehensive-policy patched

delete - delete a NetworkPolicy

Delete a specific NetworkPolicy from a namespace, removing network restrictions.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the NetworkPolicy
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace containing the NetworkPolicy
  • body: DeleteOptions - deletion options
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - grace period in seconds
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - deletion propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 202 (Status): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Service NetworkPolicy

Remove network policy from Skills service namespace.

# Delete Skills NetworkPolicy
kubectl delete networkpolicy skills-ingress-policy -n skills-service
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "skills-ingress-policy" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Service Policy with Confirmation

Delete User service NetworkPolicy after verification.

# Verify and delete User NetworkPolicy
kubectl get networkpolicy user-db-egress-policy -n user-service
kubectl delete networkpolicy user-db-egress-policy -n user-service
NAME POD-SELECTOR AGE user-db-egress-policy app=user-api 5d networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "user-db-egress-policy" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author Service Policy with Dry Run

Test deletion of Author NetworkPolicy without actually removing it.

# Dry run delete Author NetworkPolicy
kubectl delete networkpolicy author-namespace-policy -n author-service --dry-run=server
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "author-namespace-policy" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete Location Service Policy from YAML

Delete Location NetworkPolicy using the original YAML file.

# Delete Location NetworkPolicy from file
kubectl delete -f location-ipblock-policy.yaml
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "location-ipblock-policy" deleted
Example 5: Force Delete JobPost Service Policy

Immediately remove JobPost NetworkPolicy.

# Force delete JobPost NetworkPolicy
kubectl delete networkpolicy jobpost-comprehensive-policy -n jobpost-service --grace-period=0 --force
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "jobpost-comprehensive-policy" force deleted

deletecollection - delete collection of NetworkPolicy

Delete multiple NetworkPolicies in a namespace matching specified criteria.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace to delete NetworkPolicies from
  • body: DeleteOptions - deletion options
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector to filter resources
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - grace period in seconds
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector to filter resources
  • limit (in query): integer - maximum number of resources to delete
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - deletion propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All NetworkPolicies in Skills Namespace

Remove all network policies from Skills service namespace.

# Delete all NetworkPolicies in Skills namespace
kubectl delete networkpolicies --all -n skills-service
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "skills-ingress-policy" deleted
Example 2: Delete NetworkPolicies by Label in User Service

Delete User service NetworkPolicies matching specific label.

# Delete User NetworkPolicies with service label
kubectl delete networkpolicies -l service=user -n user-service
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "user-db-egress-policy" deleted
Example 3: Delete NetworkPolicies with Dry Run in Author Service

Preview which Author NetworkPolicies would be deleted.

# Dry run delete all NetworkPolicies in Author namespace
kubectl delete networkpolicies --all -n author-service --dry-run=server
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "author-namespace-policy" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete NetworkPolicies Matching Multiple Labels

Delete Location NetworkPolicies with specific label combination.

# Delete Location NetworkPolicies matching app and component labels
kubectl delete networkpolicies -l app=anihpj,component=network-security -n location-service
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "location-ipblock-policy" deleted
Example 5: Delete All anihpj Application NetworkPolicies

Remove all NetworkPolicies from all anihpj jobpost namespaces.

# Delete NetworkPolicies from all anihpj namespaces
for ns in skills-service user-service author-service location-service jobpost-service; do
  kubectl delete networkpolicies --all -n $ns
done
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "skills-ingress-policy" deleted networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "user-db-egress-policy" deleted networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "author-namespace-policy" deleted networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "location-ipblock-policy" deleted networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io "jobpost-comprehensive-policy" deleted

PodDisruptionBudget

📋 PodDisruptionBudget Overview

PodDisruptionBudgetSpec - Spec configuration
PodDisruptionBudgetStatus - Status information
PodDisruptionBudgetList - Collection of PDBs
Operations - API operations
Official Docs: PodDisruptionBudget v1 API | API Version: policy/v1

Full Description

PodDisruptionBudget is an object to define the maximum disruption that can be caused to a collection of pods during voluntary disruptions such as node maintenance, cluster upgrades, or application deployments. It ensures that a specified number or percentage of pods remain available during disruption events, maintaining service reliability for critical applications in the anihpj jobpost system.

PDBs are essential for maintaining high availability of Django services like Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost APIs. By defining minAvailable or maxUnavailable constraints, PDBs protect against excessive pod terminations during rolling updates or infrastructure maintenance, ensuring that the anihpj application continues serving requests without interruption.

Syntax

# Apply PodDisruptionBudget from YAML
kubectl apply -f poddisruptionbudget.yaml

# Get a specific PodDisruptionBudget
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget pdb-name -n namespace

# Describe PodDisruptionBudget to see current status
kubectl describe poddisruptionbudget pdb-name -n namespace

# List all PodDisruptionBudgets in namespace
kubectl get poddisruptionbudgets -n namespace

# Delete a PodDisruptionBudget
kubectl delete poddisruptionbudget pdb-name -n namespace

# Get PodDisruptionBudget in YAML format
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget pdb-name -n namespace -o yaml

# List all PodDisruptionBudgets across all namespaces
kubectl get poddisruptionbudgets --all-namespaces

YAML Structure

apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: jobpost-api-pdb
  namespace: jobpost-service
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-api
  unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: IfHealthyBudget

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: policy/v1 - API version for PodDisruptionBudget
  • kind: PodDisruptionBudget - resource type
  • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard object metadata
    • name (string): PodDisruptionBudget name
    • namespace (string): Namespace containing the PDB
    • labels (map[string]string): Key-value pairs for organization
    • annotations (map[string]string): Additional metadata
  • spec (PodDisruptionBudgetSpec): Desired behavior specification
    • minAvailable (IntOrString): Minimum pods that must remain available (mutually exclusive with maxUnavailable)
    • maxUnavailable (IntOrString): Maximum pods that can be unavailable (mutually exclusive with minAvailable)
    • selector (LabelSelector): Label query to select pods protected by this PDB
    • unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy (string): Criteria for evicting unhealthy pods (IfHealthyBudget or AlwaysAllow)
  • status (PodDisruptionBudgetStatus): Most recently observed status
    • currentHealthy (int32): Current number of healthy pods
    • desiredHealthy (int32): Minimum desired number of healthy pods
    • disruptionsAllowed (int32): Number of pod disruptions currently allowed
    • expectedPods (int32): Total number of pods counted by this PDB

Return Value

  • 200 (PodDisruptionBudget): OK
  • 201 (PodDisruptionBudget): Created
  • 202 (PodDisruptionBudget): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Service PDB with minAvailable

Create a PodDisruptionBudget ensuring at least 2 Skills API pods remain available during disruptions.

apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: skills-api-pdb
  namespace: skills-service
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: skills-api
# Apply Skills PDB
kubectl apply -f skills-api-pdb.yaml
poddisruptionbudget.policy/skills-api-pdb created
Example 2: User Service PDB with maxUnavailable

Define User service PDB allowing maximum 1 pod to be unavailable during maintenance.

apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: user-api-pdb
  namespace: user-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: user
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: user-api
  unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: IfHealthyBudget
# Create User PDB
kubectl apply -f user-api-pdb.yaml
poddisruptionbudget.policy/user-api-pdb created
Example 3: Author Service PDB with Percentage

Protect Author service by ensuring at least 80% of pods remain available.

apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: author-api-pdb
  namespace: author-service
spec:
  minAvailable: "80%"
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: author-api
      tier: backend
# Apply Author PDB with percentage
kubectl apply -f author-api-pdb.yaml
poddisruptionbudget.policy/author-api-pdb created
Example 4: Location Service PDB with AlwaysAllow Policy

Create Location service PDB with AlwaysAllow policy for faster deployments.

apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: location-api-pdb
  namespace: location-service
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 50%
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: location-api
  unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: AlwaysAllow
# Create Location PDB
kubectl apply -f location-api-pdb.yaml
poddisruptionbudget.policy/location-api-pdb created
Example 5: JobPost Service PDB with Strict Availability

Define comprehensive JobPost PDB ensuring 3 out of 5 pods always available.

apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: jobpost-api-pdb
  namespace: jobpost-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: api
    tier: backend
spec:
  minAvailable: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-api
      environment: production
  unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: IfHealthyBudget
# Apply JobPost PDB
kubectl apply -f jobpost-api-pdb.yaml
poddisruptionbudget.policy/jobpost-api-pdb created

PodDisruptionBudgetSpec

Full Description

PodDisruptionBudgetSpec defines the desired disruption budget for a set of pods. It specifies either minAvailable (minimum pods that must stay running) or maxUnavailable (maximum pods that can be down) during voluntary disruptions. The selector field identifies which pods are protected by this budget using label selectors.

The spec controls how Kubernetes handles pod evictions during maintenance operations for anihpj services. By setting minAvailable to 2 for the User API, you ensure at least 2 replicas remain operational during node drains or cluster upgrades. The unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy field determines whether unhealthy pods can be evicted, providing additional control over service availability during disruption events.

Syntax
# Get PodDisruptionBudget spec
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget pdb-name -n namespace -o jsonpath='{.spec}'

# View minAvailable setting
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget pdb-name -n namespace -o jsonpath='{.spec.minAvailable}'

# Check selector configuration
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget pdb-name -n namespace -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector}'
Parameters Description
  • minAvailable (IntOrString): Minimum number or percentage of pods that must remain available (mutually exclusive with maxUnavailable). Can be an integer (e.g., 2) or string percentage (e.g., "80%")
  • maxUnavailable (IntOrString): Maximum number or percentage of pods that can be unavailable (mutually exclusive with minAvailable)
  • selector (LabelSelector, required): Label query to select pods whose evictions are managed by this disruption budget. Null selector matches no pods, empty selector ({}) selects all pods
  • unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy (string): Defines criteria for evicting unhealthy pods
    • IfHealthyBudget: Unhealthy pods can be evicted only if budget allows (default)
    • AlwaysAllow: All unhealthy pods can be evicted regardless of budget
Return Value

Returns a PodDisruptionBudgetSpec object containing disruption budget configuration including availability constraints, pod selector, and eviction policy.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Skills Service Spec with Integer minAvailable

View Skills PDB spec showing integer minAvailable constraint.

# Get Skills PDB spec
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget skills-api-pdb -n skills-service -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq
{ "minAvailable": 2, "selector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "skills-api" } } }
Example 2: User Service Spec with maxUnavailable

Display User PDB spec showing maxUnavailable and eviction policy.

# Describe User PDB to see spec details
kubectl describe poddisruptionbudget user-api-pdb -n user-service
Name: user-api-pdb Namespace: user-service Labels: app=anihpj service=user Spec: Max unavailable: 1 Selector: app=user-api Unhealthy Pod Eviction Policy: IfHealthyBudget Status: Current Healthy: 3 Desired Healthy: 2 Disruptions Allowed: 1 Expected pods: 3
Example 3: Author Service Percentage-based Spec

Extract Author PDB spec showing percentage-based minAvailable.

# Get Author PDB minAvailable value
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget author-api-pdb -n author-service -o yaml | grep -A 5 spec:
spec: minAvailable: 80% selector: matchLabels: app: author-api tier: backend
Example 4: Location Service Selector Configuration

View Location PDB selector matching specific labels.

# Get Location PDB selector
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget location-api-pdb -n location-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector}' | jq
{ "matchLabels": { "app": "location-api" } }
Example 5: JobPost Service Complete Spec

Display full JobPost PDB spec with all configuration options.

# Get JobPost PDB complete spec
kubectl get poddisruptionbudget jobpost-api-pdb -n jobpost-service -o json | jq '.spec'
{ "minAvailable": 3, "selector": { "matchLabels": { "app": "jobpost-api", "environment": "production" } }, "unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy": "IfHealthyBudget" }

PodDisruptionBudgetStatus

Full Description

PodDisruptionBudgetStatus represents information about the status of a PodDisruptionBudget. It provides real-time visibility into the health and availability of pods managed by the PDB, including the current number of healthy pods, desired healthy count, and how many disruptions are currently allowed. Status may trail the actual state of the system but reflects the most recent observation.
